Get Started — Internal

Meeting Guidelines

Standard formats and guidelines for sending meeting invitations across change request and non-change request discussions.

Meeting Title Formats

A. Change Request

1

Internal Change Request Presentation ICRP

Format [Ticket No.] [Project Name] [High Level Description] – ICRP
Example VOYW-000001 Voyadores Incident Report – ICRP
2

External Change Request Presentation ECRP

Format [Ticket No.] [Project Name] [High Level Description] – ECRP
Example VOYW-000001 Voyadores Incident Report – ECRP
3

Change Request Requirement Discussion CRRD

Format [Ticket No.] [Project Name] [High Level Description] – CRRD
Example VOYW-000001 Voyadores Incident Report – CRRD
4

Change Request Design Discussion CRDD

Format [Ticket No.] [Project Name] [High Level Description] – CRDD
Example VOYW-000001 Voyadores Incident Report – CRDD
5

Change Request Technical Discussion CRTD

Format [Ticket No.] [Project Name] [High Level Description] – CRTD
Example VOYW-000001 Voyadores Incident Report – CRTD

B. Non-Change Request Internal Discussion

Format [Project Name / Team Name] [High Level Description] Discussion
Examples
Admin Team Building 2024 Discussion Infra Application Migration Discussion

C. Non-Change Request External Discussion

Format [Project Name / Team Name] [High Level Description] Discussion
Example eSuite Unified Contract Discussion

Change Request Requirements Discussion CRRD

Purpose

Collaborate and analyze business processes and objectives. Participants focus on analyzing the request workflow, feature behavior, and the overall impact of the changes on the application. Permission in terms of feature accessibility and data should also be discussed.

Meeting Setup

Scheduled after the business analysts gather the final requirements from the client.

Meeting Title CRRD – [Ticket No.] [Short Description]

Participants

Required
Heads Leads Business Analysts Systems Analysts
Optional
Systems Engineers UI/UX Engineers

Agenda Items

  1. Scope Identification
  2. Feature Behaviour
  3. Workflow
  4. Feature Role Permission
  5. Data Permission
  6. Impact Analysis

Agenda Item Explanations

Scope Identification

An essential step in project management involving defining the boundaries and objectives of the project. By clearly identifying and documenting the project's deliverables, requirements, and constraints, the scope provides a foundation for successful project execution.

Feature Behaviour

How a particular feature or functionality behaves within a system or application — the expected actions, responses, and outcomes when using or interacting with that specific feature.

Workflow

The sequence of steps or activities that need to be followed to complete a task or achieve a specific outcome. It outlines the logical order and dependencies between different tasks or processes within a system or organization.

Feature Role Permission

Access rights and privileges within a system or application determining what actions or functions each role can perform and what data they can access or modify. The definition and identification of Feature Roles should be done by Developers.

Guidelines in Feature Role creation

  1. Single page scope
  2. Generally divided into two functions:
    • Editor
    • Viewer
  3. If functionality requires a different set of permissions, a custom Feature Role will be created.
Data Permission

Access and security controls placed on data within a system or application — specifying who can view, edit, or delete data and what level of access different user roles have to specific data sets.

Impact Analysis

Assessing the potential consequences and effects of a proposed change or action within a system or project. It examines how a change in one area may impact other components, processes, or stakeholders and helps evaluate potential risks and mitigation strategies.

Output

Sequence Diagrams

A visual blueprint illustrating the precise order and manner in which various components or functions within a software application communicate and collaborate, particularly when making enhancements or modifications.

Word Excel Visio Lucid Chart
Updated Changelog

An informal document of a Change Request — an in-between step between client requirements (email, chats, or documents) and a Business Requirements Document (BRD). Participants create a concise and comprehensive changelog to assist developers in understanding and implementing the required changes.

OneNote
Resource Assignment

Helps the team with better allocation planning, ensuring that assigned resources can handle the task without neglecting other responsibilities. By the end of the meeting the team should be able to determine the specific resources needed to implement the change effectively.

Internal Timeline

An internal schedule to which development activities will be delivered.

Change Request Design Discussion CRDD

Purpose

Collaborate, evaluate, and discuss proposed changes or enhancements to the user interface (UI) and user experience (UX) of the application.

Meeting Setup

Scheduled after the Change Request Requirements Discussion and before the Change Request Technical Discussion.

Meeting Title CRDD – [Ticket No.] [Short Description]

Participants

Required
Head of UI/UX UI/UX Engineers Designers
Optional
Business Analysts Systems Analysts Systems Engineers

Agenda Items

  • User Interface
    • Visual Design
    • Navigation
    • Page Layout
    • Responsive Design
    • Icons and Imagery
  • User Experience
    • View Structure
      • Page
      • Dialog
    • Control
    • Libraries
    • Validation
    • Theme
    • Style

Agenda Item Explanations

User Experience (UX)

Focuses on creating a positive and intuitive experience for users interacting with a product, service, or system. It involves understanding user needs, preferences, and behaviors to design interfaces that are easy to use, visually appealing, and satisfying.

View Structure

The organization and arrangement of different screens or views within an application — the hierarchical relationship between screens and how users navigate through them. A well-designed view structure enhances usability and allows users to easily access and navigate the desired content or functionality.

  • Page — A distinct screen or view within an application representing a specific section or functionality, containing various components and elements that enable user interaction and information display.
  • Dialog — A small, separate window or pop-up that appears on top of the main page. Used to capture user input, display additional information, or prompt for confirmation or decision-making.
Controls

Interactive elements or components that allow users to interact with the application. Examples include buttons, checkboxes, dropdown lists, input fields, sliders, and menus — enabling users to input data, select options, initiate actions, or navigate through the application.

Libraries

Pre-built sets of UI components or frameworks that provide ready-made solutions for designing and implementing user interfaces. These libraries offer a collection of reusable elements, styles, and functionalities for creating consistent and visually appealing user experiences.

Validation

Checking and ensuring that user input or data meets specific requirements or constraints — verifying the accuracy, completeness, and integrity of user-provided information. Validation helps prevent errors, improve data quality, and provide feedback to users when their input does not meet specified criteria.

Theme

The visual appearance and styling of an application's user interface — including color schemes, typography, iconography, and overall visual aesthetics. Themes help create a consistent and visually appealing look and feel across different screens and components.

Style

Specific visual attributes and properties applied to user interface elements such as buttons, text, backgrounds, and borders — including font choices, color combinations, spacing, alignment, and overall visual presentation. Consistent styling enhances the user experience by providing a cohesive and visually pleasing interface.

Output

Mockups

Visual representations or prototypes depicting the proposed user interface or system behavior. The UI/UX team creates mockups after the discussion to enable both the team and the client to visualize the expected outcome of the change.

Figma
Updated Changelog

An informal document of a Change Request — an in-between step between the client's requirements (in any form such as email, chats, or documents) to a Business Requirements Document (BRD).

OneNote

Change Request Presentation

A

Internal Change Request Presentation ICRP

Purpose

Discuss the high-level impact of the change request on the application. By the end of the presentation, participants should have a clear understanding of the new application process once the change request has been implemented.

Meeting Setup

Scheduled after gathering the requirements from the client.

Meeting Title ICRP – [Ticket No.] [High Level Description]

Participants

Required
Heads Leads Business Analysts Systems Analysts
Optional
Systems Engineers UI/UX Engineers

Agenda Items

  1. Change Request Items
  2. Current Process
  3. Proposed Process
  4. Timeline

Agenda Item Explanations

Change Request Items

The things or elements that people want to modify or improve in a particular system or application.

Current Process

Represented through a diagram or flow chart illustrating the workflow of an application or system before any changes or improvements are made. Helps the client understand the activities or processes at a high level through visual representation.

Proposed Process

Represented through a diagram or flow chart. Provides the client with a broader understanding of the change request's impact when implemented in a system or application.

Timeline

The schedule for implementing the proposed changes, outlining the sequence of activities and estimated timeframes for completing each step of the change request process.

  1. Requirements Gathering
  2. Initiate Development
  3. Internal Testing
  4. UAT
  5. Go or No Go
  6. Production Deployment

Output

Change Request Presentation

A presentation conducted to discuss and present the proposed change request for a system or application, communicating the details of the change request, its objectives, and the impact it will have on the project or system.

B

External Change Request Presentation ECRP

Purpose

Present and discuss the Change Request presentation with clients, providing them with an overview of the impact of the change request on the application.

Meeting Title ECRP – [Ticket No.] [High Level Description]

Participants

Required
Heads Leads Business Analysts Systems Analysts Clients

Presentation Types

1
Pre-Deployment Presentation

Provided to clients before the deployment of a change request or enhancement feature to production.

2
Post-Deployment Presentation

Provided to clients after the deployment of a change request or enhancement feature to production.

On this page
